region_of_origin: Central and South America (widely cultivated globally)

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated black bean code; A00LC (dried beans, raw) is the closest defensible parent node for Phaseolus vulgaris var. black.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of black beans is approximately 60g raw (yielding roughly 150g cooked after soaking and boiling), commonly used in soups, stews, rice dishes, or salads.

nutrient_source.title: Black beans, mature seeds, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173735); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database (F09020003)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo (F09020003, confidence 0.95). Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for black beans, mature seeds, raw (FDC ID 173735). Values are consistent between both approved sources. HPB values used as primary given Singapore database provenance.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; black beans (boiled) GI ~30
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 30 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the peer-reviewed publication by Atkinson, Foster-Powell & Brand-Miller (Diabetes Care, 2008) for black beans (boiled). Raw black beans are not directly tested; the GI value applies to the cooked form, which is the relevant state for consumption. GL per serving calculated as: GI (30) × available carbohydrate per raw serving (60g raw × 0.6236 carb = 37.4g available carb, adjusted for cooking yield to ~23g per 150g cooked serving) / 100 = 30 × 37.4 / 100 = 11.2, rounded to 11.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally high potassium-to-sodium ratio (1483mg K : 5mg Na per 100g raw) with high dietary fibre supporting vascular health
hypertension.guidance: Black beans are highly suitable for hypertensive individuals; their negligible sodium (5mg/100g) and very high potassium (1483mg/100g) support blood pressure regulation consistent with DASH dietary guidelines — avoid canned varieties with added salt and rinse thoroughly if using canned beans.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30 cooked), high dietary fibre (15.5g/100g raw) and high protein (21.6g/100g raw) significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite high total carbohydrate in the raw form (62.4g/100g), black beans have a very low GI of approximately 30 when cooked, making them appropriate for type 2 diabetic meal plans at a portion of approximately 150g cooked; patients should note that raw values overstate carbohydrate density relative to the cooked serving and should avoid canned varieties with added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High soluble fibre, very low saturated fat (0.37g/100g), zero cholesterol, and high potassium with anthocyanin polyphenols supporting lipid and vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Black beans are cardioprotective: their high soluble fibre content supports LDL cholesterol reduction, negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol pose no atherogenic risk, and their anthocyanin pigments provide antioxidant and anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to cardiovascular disease prevention.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols (anthocyanins), high fibre supporting gut-immune modulation, calcium and iron important for RA patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Black beans are suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to their anti-inflammatory anthocyanin content and high fibre; ensure thorough cooking as raw or undercooked black beans contain lectins that may worsen gut inflammation, and avoid preparation with lard or high-sodium seasonings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black bean (raw) refers to the dried, uncooked seeds of Phaseolus vulgaris (black variety), a legume characterised by a deep purple-black seed coat, firm dry texture, and mild earthy flavour. Raw black beans must be soaked (typically 8–12 hours) and thoroughly boiled before consumption, as raw or undercooked beans contain lectins and phytates that can cause gastrointestinal distress and impair nutrient absorption. Per 100g raw, black beans provide 341 kcal, 21.6g protein, 1.42g fat, 62.4g carbohydrate, 15.5g dietary fibre, and only 5mg sodium — making them one of the most nutrient-dense plant protein sources available. Their exceptionally high potassium content (1483mg/100g) and negligible sodium give them one of the highest potassium-to-sodium ratios of any food, strongly supporting DASH diet compliance for hypertension management. Despite high raw carbohydrate values, the glycemic index of cooked black beans is approximately 30 (low GI), making them appropriate for type 2 diabetic meal plans at a portion of approximately 150g cooked, owing to the combined effect of high fibre and protein slowing glucose absorption. Black beans are rich in folate (444mcg DFE/100g), phosphorus (352mg), magnesium (171mg), iron (5.02mg), and calcium (123mg). Their characteristic black pigment is due to anthocyanins, which confer antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Black beans are widely used in Latin American, Caribbean, and increasingly Southeast Asian cuisines, appearing in soups, stews, rice dishes, burritos, and salads. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, they are suitable for type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis management when properly cooked.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database (F09020003, confidence 0.95, reviewed status) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173735) for black beans, mature seeds, raw. Both approved sources are in close agreement. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. (2008) publication. Ontology anchors are defensible. All mandatory fields are complete.
notes: 1. All per_100g values represent the raw, dried, uncooked state. Cooked black beans have significantly lower nutrient density per 100g due to water absorption (approximately 2.5× weight increase on cooking); cooked values are approximately: 132 kcal, 8.9g protein, 23g carbohydrate, 7.5g fibre per 100g cooked. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw black beans). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — black beans are a plant food containing no cholesterol). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no significant vitamin D in raw legumes). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy for dried raw black beans). 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae and vitamin_e_mg are set to -1.0: detailed retinol activity equivalent and tocopherol data for raw black beans are not consistently reported in the HPB Singapore database; USDA SR Legacy reports small amounts but values vary by variety — flagged for future update. 4. GI value of 30 applies to cooked black beans; no direct GI measurement exists for raw black beans as they are not consumed raw. GL per serving calculated using 60g raw serving yielding approximately 37.4g available carbohydrate (raw basis): GL = 30 × 37.4 / 100 = 11.2 (reported as 11.3). 5. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible parent node; a dedicated black bean FoodEx2 code was not identified in the publicly available FoodEx2 catalogue as of April 2026. 6. Saturated fat (0.37g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.12g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.61g), and additional micronutrient values (B-vitamins, minerals) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173735) where not reported in HPB, consistent with approved source policy.
